creator: tedm@ipinc.net
text:
This is a known issue with ALL low end HP Proliant servers that lack an actual hardware RAID card and INCLUDES operating systems other than FreeBSD.  For example a HP Proliant Gen 9 DL20 if the BIOS is configured into MBR Boot mode and SATA disks configured as generic ATA devices, Windows Server 2019 will not recognize that disks are attached even with the HP-supplied disk device driver.  If the system is chanted to UEFI boot and the RAID is configured via BIOS and SAS disks are used, it WILL recognize the array as a hardware device, if SATA disks are used in the same configuration, it will require the HP "fakeraid" driver.

These servers were built by HP for the "I work hard to shoot myself in the foot crowd"  They were targeted at the extreme low-end SOHO / SMB market, who rub the face off every nickel, and were trying to keep that crowd with some semblance of data integrity.  They all intended the target customer to use 2 hard drives in a mirrored configuration running software "fake" RAID and they assumed that the installers didn't know software RAID from insect killer.

Low end HP Proliant servers often come with what HP refers to as "hybrid RAID" chips on-motherboard.  Depending on the disks used, modes the BIOS puts the disks into, and whether UEFI or MBR booting is configured in BIOS, and whether the RAID chip is configured RAID 1 or RAID 5, the chip may present as simple ATA devices detectible by any operating system, or "fakeraid" volumes that are only detectible by loading the dmraid driver (under Linux) or HP's driver (under Windows) or actual hardware RAID volumes that are usable by any OS.

There is no one-size-fits-all method of configuring a low end HP Proliant server BIOS to guarantee success with any given operating system.  HP's by-the-book method is to use it's Smart Installer which knows about all the various permutations the different operating systems require, and BIOS configurations required, of course this only works with a very limited number of operating systems, for example a ML110 purchased for use with Windows Server 2012 may work with the Smart Installer but that installer may not support Server 2022 depending on the generation of the server.

It's strongly recommended to buy an actual hardware RAID card and install it in any of these lower end servers (DL20, ML110, etc.) and use that instead of the onboard BIOS chip which is hit or miss under ALL operating systems (not just Linux and FreeBSD)

In recent years Linux has been modified so that most of the older instructions for inserting dmraid (the fakeraid driver that detects the BIOS fakeraid signatures on the disks and allows dual booting) don't work, FreeBSD similarly has moved on from the older wd driver that made software RAID on bootable media ridiculously easy.
